title: Kawasaki Disease
oneLiner: Acute, self-limited vasculitis of medium-sized arteries, primarily in children <5 years, causing coronary artery aneurysms if untreated.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 3-year-old boy presents with day 6 of high spiking fever (39.5°C) unresponsive to paracetamol and ibuprofen. Mum notes he has been irritable, refusing food, and has red, cracked lips. On examination you find bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, a polymorphous truncal rash, erythema of the palms and soles with mild swelling, and a single 2 cm right anterior cervical lymph node. The child is miserable, and the parents are exhausted.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues and what they tell you:**

- **Fever ≥5 days (day 6 here):** This is the *sine qua non* — no fever, no Kawasaki. The duration reflects ongoing systemic inflammation; fever persisting beyond 5 days is the diagnostic gateway.
- **Irritability out of proportion:** Suggests CNS inflammation (aseptic meningitis) or severe general malaise. In a febrile child, extreme irritability is a red flag for vasculitis.
- **Red, cracked lips and strawberry tongue:** Mucosal involvement — hyperaemia of oral mucosa, prominent papillae. The vasculitis targets small- to medium-sized vessels in mucous membranes.
- **Bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection:** Not "pink eye" (no discharge, no crusting). Vasculitis of bulbar conjunctival vessels; hints at systemic endothelial activation.
- **Polymorphous rash:** Often maculopapular, can be scarlatiniform or target-like. Not diagnostic alone, but supports acute inflammation.
- **Erythema and swelling of palms/soles:** "Strawberry hands and feet" — acute-phase vasodilation and oedema. In subacute phase (weeks 2-3), you'll see periungual desquamation.
- **Single enlarged cervical node >1.5 cm:** Usually non-suppurative, unilateral. Least common criterion (present in ~50-75%). The node is a red herring for bacterial lymphadenitis, but cultures are negative.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Incomplete Kawasaki Disease (IKD):** Fever ≥5 days but only 2-3 of the other 5 criteria. Commonest in infants <6 months and older children >5 years. Look for supporting labs (elevated CRP, ESR, low albumin, sterile pyuria) and echocardiogram changes.
- **Infant <6 months:** May present only with fever and irritability, no classic signs at all. High silent coronary aneurysm rate — **echocardiogram is mandatory** for any febrile infant with no source and high inflammatory markers.
- **Older child (>5 years):** More likely to have atypical features like cervical lymphadenopathy mimicking bacterial infection, or abdominal pain from vasculitis of mesenteric vessels. They also have higher rates of IVIG resistance.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Kawasaki disease (KD) is an acute, self-limited inflammation of blood vessel walls (vasculitis) that specifically targets the coronary arteries. It mostly strikes young children, and if not treated within 10 days of fever onset, it can cause balloon-like enlargements (aneurysms) in the heart's arteries.

**Why it matters clinically:**  

- **Frequency:** Leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in developed countries (after congenital heart disease). Incidence ~20/100,000 in Japan, ~9-19/100,000 in UK/US, with peak at 1-2 years.
- **Stakes:** Untreated, 15-25% develop coronary artery aneurysms; even with treatment, risk falls to ~4%. Aneurysms can thrombose → myocardial infarction, rupture, or cause ischaemic heart disease in young adults.
- **Common pitfalls:**  
  - Misdiagnosing as viral exanthem, scarlet fever, or measles → delayed treatment.  
  - Missing incomplete KD in infants → silent aneurysms.  
  - Forgetting that fever must be **documented** for 5 days (history counts).  
  - Not arranging early echocardiogram (day 7-10 is optimal, but can be earlier).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of how KD develops:**

KD is triggered by an unknown antigen (likely a microbial superantigen) in a genetically susceptible host (certain HLA types, Asian ethnicity). The antigen activates T cells and macrophages → massive cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-8) → systemic inflammation.

Vascular endothelium expresses adhesion molecules, attracting neutrophils and later macrophages. In medium-sized arteries (especially coronaries), neutrophils infiltrate the vessel wall and release proteases, causing destruction of the internal elastic lamina and smooth muscle. This weakens the vessel wall → dilatation → aneurysm.

**Why each clinical sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

- **Fever:** Cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) reset the hypothalamic setpoint → sustained high body temperature.
- **Conjunctival injection:** Vasodilation and inflammation of bulbar conjunctival vessels (no discharge because it's not an infection of the conjunctival epithelium).
- **Mucosal changes (red lips, strawberry tongue):** Vasculitis of oral mucosal vessels causes hyperaemia, oedema, and later desquamation. The tongue's papillae become prominent due to oedema and inflammation.
- **Rash:** Dermal small-vessel vasculitis → polymorphous exanthem. Not specific, but the pattern (truncal, perineal accentuation) hints at KD.
- **Extremity changes (erythema, oedema, desquamation):** Acute: vasodilation and dermal oedema from cytokine-mediated capillary leak. Subacute (weeks 2-3): ischaemia of the superficial epidermis due to small-vessel involvement → periungual desquamation.
- **Cervical lymphadenopathy:** Drainage from inflamed pharyngeal or upper respiratory mucosa; the node is reactive, not suppurative.
- **Irritability:** CNS vasculitis or meningeal inflammation (CSF shows pleocytosis in ~30%).
- **Coronary aneurysms:** Medium-sized artery wall destruction. The same process can affect other medium arteries (axillary, renal, mesenteric) but coronaries are the clinical focus.

**Connect pathophys to pattern-recognition cues:**  

- Fever ≥5 days = sustained cytokine release (not a typical viral pattern that resolves by day 3-4).  
- Bilateral conjunctivitis without discharge = endothelial activation, not bacterial conjunctivitis (which is purulent).  
- Strawberry tongue = mucosal vasculitis, not Group A Strep pharyngitis (which is exudative and painful).  
- Swollen hands/feet = capillary leak + local vasodilation, not oedema from renal or cardiac causes (no proteinuria or heart failure).  
- Irritability = CNS involvement → think beyond viral meningitis (no photophobia or neck stiffness typical of viral or bacterial).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category        | Specific Trigger / Factor                         | Stem Clue                                                    | Most Common      |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic**  | Unknown, but thought to be infection-triggered in genetically predisposed | No clear contact; child otherwise well; starts abruptly       | **Yes — over 90% of cases** |
| **Infectious**  | Superantigen-producing pathogens (Staph, Strep, Mycoplasma, EBV, Adenovirus) | Prodromal URI or GI symptoms; clustering in winter/spring; sometimes sibling cases | May be co-trigger, not single cause |
| **Genetic**     | HLA class II polymorphisms, ITPKC gene variants   | Asian ethnicity (especially Japanese, Korean); family history | MODIFIER, not cause |
| **Environmental**| Possibly carpet shampoo, mercury, detergents (weak evidence) | Rare; historical reports only                                  | Not clinically useful |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **idiopathic** — you will almost never find a single pathogen. Just treat empirically.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (American Heart Association, 2017):**  

- **Classic KD:** Fever ≥5 days + at least 4 of 5 principal clinical features:  
  1. Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection  
  2. Oral changes (red lips, strawberry tongue, pharyngeal erythema)  
  3. Polymorphous rash  
  4. Extremity changes (erythema/oedema of palms/soles; later periungual desquamation)  
  5. Cervical lymphadenopathy >1.5 cm (usually unilateral)  

- **Incomplete KD:** Fever ≥5 days + <4 features, but **echocardiogram shows coronary changes** OR lab findings (elevated CRP/ESR + ≥3 supplemental lab criteria).  

**Supplemental lab criteria:**  
- Albumin ≤3.0 g/dL  
- Anaemia for age  
- ALT >50 U/L  
- Platelets ≥450 x 10⁹/L after day 7 (thrombocytosis is *late* — not helpful acutely)  
- WBC ≥15 x 10⁹/L  
- Urine WBC >10/hpf (sterile pyuria)

**Pattern-recognition:** No single lab confirms KD; diagnosis is clinical + lab support. But **echo is the gold standard** – if you see coronary artery dilation or aneurysms, you have KD regardless of criteria.

| Test            | What It Shows                                           | Why Order                 | How to Interpret                                                                                                                                 |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| CRP             | Highly elevated >30-100 mg/L (normal <5)                 | Marker of systemic inflammation | KD typically has very high CRP (often >70). Falling CRP after IVIG is a good sign.                                                              |
| ESR             | Elevated >40 mm/hr (can be >100)                        | Same, but slower          | ESR may remain high for weeks; not useful for monitoring acute response.                                                                          |
| WBC with diff   | Leukocytosis, neutrophilia                              | Acute-phase               | >15 is supportive, but not specific.                                                                                                             |
| Platelets       | Normal in first week; **thrombocytosis** weeks 2-3       | Late diagnostic clue       | Platelets >450 after day 7 is highly suggestive. If you see this, re-evaluate for missed KD.                                                     |
| Albumin         | Low (<3.0 g/dL)                                         | Capillary leak             | Hypoalbuminaemia is common in KD (loss into tissues). Supports diagnosis.                                                                        |
| LFT (ALT)       | Mildly elevated (AST/ALT 2-3x normal)                   | Hepatic involvement        | Transaminitis is common (~40%). Suggests systemic vasculitis.                                                                                    |
| Urinalysis      | Sterile pyuria (>10 WBC/hpf, no bacteria)                | Rule out UTI               | Indicates renal vasculitis. If positive, don't treat as UTI – consider KD.                                                                       |
| Echo (echocardiogram) | Coronary artery Z-scores (diameter adjusted for BSA) | **Definitive** for aneurysms | Z-score ≥2.5 = aneurysm. Repeat at 1-2 weeks, then 6-8 weeks after onset.                                                                        |

**Single finding that confirms KD:**  
No single test — but a **coronary artery aneurysm on echocardiogram** in a child with fever ≥5 days is pathognomonic. Most cases don't have aneurysms at presentation, so the clinical criteria and labs guide early treatment.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **IVIG** (2 g/kg as single IV infusion over 10-12 hours) — reduces coronary aneurysm rate from 25% to 4% if given within 10 days of fever onset. Mechanism: anti-inflammatory, neutralises superantigens, downregulates cytokines.  
- **Aspirin** — high-dose initially (30-50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h) until afebrile for 48-72 hours. Anti-inflammatory dose. Start at same time as IVIG.  
- **Paracetamol** — if fever high (but don't mask response to IVIG).  
- **Check IVIG resistance risk factors:** severe hypoalbuminaemia, very high CRP, low platelets, elevated bilirubin. If high risk, consider adding corticosteroids early (discuss with senior).

**Ward (24-72 hours):**  
- **Monitor for defervescence** — if fever persists ≥36 hours after IVIG, this is **IVIG resistance** (occurring in ~10-20%). Needs second dose IVIG ± methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg IV for 1-3 days (or infliximab 5 mg/kg IV).  
- **Transition aspirin to low-dose** (3-5 mg/kg/day once daily) once child is afebrile and CRP falling. Low-dose aspirin for antiplatelet effect — decreases thrombosis risk on any developing aneurysm.  
- **Echocardiogram** — ideally day 7-10 after onset (not day 1, because aneurysms take time to develop). Earlier if critically ill.  
- **Daily monitoring of CRP, clinical signs, fluid balance.** KD kids are often dehydrated from fever and poor intake.

**Long-term:**  
- **Aspirin (low-dose) continued** until echocardiogram at 6-8 weeks shows normal coronaries. If normal, stop aspirin. If aneurysm persists, continue aspirin indefinitely ± warfarin if giant aneurysm (>8 mm).  
- **Cardiology follow-up** — serial EKGs, stress tests, angiography if aneurysms present.  
- **Live vaccines** — defer MMR/varicella for 11 months after IVIG (circulating antibodies interfere).  
- **Lifelong risk** — even regressed aneurysms may have impaired vasodilation; adult risk of accelerated atherosclerosis.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**  
- **Do NOT use corticosteroids as first-line** — they increase aneurysm risk in KD (except in IVIG-resistant cases, or as adjunct in high-risk patients).  
- **Do NOT stop aspirin early** — antiplatelet effect needed for 6-8 weeks even with normal echo (may have subtle endothelial dysfunction).  
- **Do NOT give NSAIDs (ibuprofen)** — compete with aspirin for COX-1 binding, reduce antiplatelet effect. Use paracetamol.  
- **Do NOT use IVIG if >10 days of fever and no echo evidence of aneurysm** — but if aneurysm present, still give IVIG to reduce inflammation.  
- **Do NOT forget echo** — a normal echo at day 7 does not rule out later aneurysm; repeat at 6-8 weeks.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature          | Kawasaki Disease (KD)      | Scarlet Fever (Group A Strep) | Measles                  | Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA) – systemic onset | Viral Exanthem (e.g., Adenovirus) |
|------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Pain**         | Irritability, no localised pain | Sore throat, headache        | Cough, coryza, photophobia | Arthralgia/arthritis (can be migratory)              | Malignant fever, aches            |
| **Exam**         | Conjunctivitis (non-purulent), red lips, strawberry tongue, palmar/plantar erythema, rash, unilateral cervical node | Circumoral pallor, strawberry tongue (early), red pharynx with exudate, sandpaper rash, Pastia lines, desquamation later | Three Cs: Cough, Coryza, Conjunctivitis (purulent). Koplik spots (buccal, white). Rash: confluent, red, starting on face. | High quotidian fever (once or twice daily), evanescent salmon-pink rash, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly | Pharyngitis, tonsillitis, exudates sometimes; follicular conjunctivitis only. No strawberry tongue or palmar changes. |
| **Oral findings** | Red cracked lips, strawberry tongue, pharyngeal erythema without exudates | Strawberry tongue (early white, later red), exudative pharyngitis | Koplik spots (white on red base, buccal), then red throat | Usually normal oral mucosa | Pharyngeal injection, vesicles/herpangina possible |
| **Extremities**  | Palmar/plantar erythema, oedema, **periungual desquamation** (weeks 2-3) | Desquamation (late, around flexures, fingers) | No specific extremity findings | Joint swelling, warmth, limited range | No specific extremity changes |
| **Lymph nodes**  | Single anterior cervical node >1.5 cm, non-suppurative | Tender, suppurative cervical nodes | Posterior auricular, occipital nodes | Generalised adenopathy | Cervical nodes, often bilateral, may be reactive |
| **ECG**          | May show myocarditis (ST changes), arrhythmias | Normal              | Normal                  | Pericarditis/myocarditis possible                    | Normal                          |
| **Labs**         | High CRP, high ESR, low albumin, thrombocytosis (later), sterile pyuria | ASOT, anti-DNAse B positive, culture + throat | Measles IgM/IgG, viral PCR | Anaemia, high CRP/ESR, ANA/ RF negative usually | Viral PCR, serology; low CRP often |
| **Key differentiator** | Fever ≥5 days + 4/5 criteria + coronary aneurysm on echo | Strep culture + ASOT; responds to penicillin | Koplik spots + measles PCR; prodrome + face-first rash | Arthritis + typical quotidian fever + rash; responds to NSAIDs/systemic steroids | Self-limited (3-5 days), normal echo, no conjunctivitis or oral changes typical |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complication #1: Coronary artery aneurysms (CAA)**  
- **How it presents:** Often asymptomatic initially. On echo: z-score ≥2.5 (mild) to giant aneurysm (≥8 mm). Giant aneurysms may present later as ischaemia, dysrhythmia, or sudden death.  
- **Key signs:** None until thrombotic event: chest pain, pallor, syncope, cardiogenic shock in a child with history of fever weeks ago. ECG: ST elevation, Q waves (myocardial infarction).  
- **What to do:** Urgent echo, ECG, troponin. If acute MI, manage per paediatric cardiology protocol (thrombolysis or angioplasty tricky in children). Immediate heparin, consult cardiology.  

**Major complication #2: Myocarditis**  
- **How it presents:** Heart failure (tachypnoea, gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, hypotension) within first week.  
- **Key signs:** Tachycardia out of proportion to fever, pulsus alternans, raised JVP.  
- **What to do:** ECG (low voltage, ST/T changes), echo (reduced ejection fraction), treat with IVIG, diuretics, inotropes (ICU).  

**Major complication #3: Aseptic meningitis**  
- **How it presents:** Irritability, photophobia, headache.  
- **Key signs:** Meningism but CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, negative cultures.  
- **What to do:** Lumbar puncture (to rule out bacterial meningitis). Treat underlying KD — resolves with IVIG.  

**Major complication #4: Peripheral arterial aneurysms**  
- **How it presents:** Pulsatile masses in axilla, groin, or abdomen. Rare but can rupture.  
- **What to do:** Vascular surgery referral.  

**Late-presentation patterns (the STEMs testing "what happens if untreated"):**  
- "A 7-year-old child presents with syncope while running. Had a prolonged febrile illness at age 2 not fully evaluated." — Think old Kawasaki with giant aneurysm causing exertion-induced ischaemia.  
- "A 20-year-old adult has an MI with no risk factors. Echocardiogram shows a calcified coronary aneurysm." — Sequelae of undiagnosed KD in childhood.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"CRASH"** — the 5 clinical features (non-fever criteria):  
   - **C**onjunctivitis (bilateral, non-purulent)  
   - **R**ash (polymorphous, truncal)  
   - **A**denopathy (cervical, >1.5 cm, unilateral)  
   - **S**trawberry tongue / oral changes  
   - **H**ands/feet changes (erythema, oedema, later desquamation)  

2. **"5 days of fever, 4 of 5 signs"** — diagnostic threshold for classic KD.

3. **"Ideal to IVIG before day 10"** — give IVIG ideally day 7-10; after day 10, still give if echo abnormal.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

- In a febrile child with no source, **always ask about conjunctival injection and check palms/ soles** — the classic exam is frequently missed.
- **Periungual desquamation** (fingertip peeling) appears **after** fever resolves, weeks 2-3. If you see this in a child with previous febrile illness, ask about the history and echo retrospectively.
- **Thrombocytosis** (>450) after day 7 is a strong clue for KD. If you see it in a febrile child with no clear diagnosis, think of incomplete KD.
- **Sterile pyuria** in a febrile child with negative urine culture — suspect KD, not UTI.
- **Age extremes:** Infants <6 months and children >5 years are atypical presentations — lower threshold for echo.
- **High-risk for IVIG resistance:** Asian male, age <1 year, low albumin, low sodium, high CRP, elevated bilirubin. Consider adding corticosteroid early.
- **Do not use ibuprofen** in KD — it competes with aspirin for COX-1 and reduces antiplatelet efficacy. Paracetamol is safe for fever.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Diagnosing scarlet fever without culture — but KD also has strawberry tongue. Look for conjunctival injection (absent in scarlet fever) and palmar oedema.
- Stopping aspirin after fever resolves — continue low-dose until 6-8 week echo confirms normal coronaries.
- Missing incomplete KD: fever + 2-3 criteria + abnormal labs = treat. Don't wait for full criteria.
- Forgetting that IVIG contains live antibody → live vaccines delayed 11 months.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / cardiology / ICU:**

- **Persistent fever >36 hours after first IVIG dose** — likely IVIG resistance; needs repeat IVIG ± steroids/infliximab. Call paediatric rheumatologist/cardiologist.
- **Signs of myocarditis or heart failure** — tachypnoea, tachycardia out of proportion, gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, low urine output, hypotension. Call ICU and cardiology.
- **Chest pain or syncope** — suspect myocardial ischaemia from aneurysm. Call cardiology emergently.
- **Hypotension not responding to bolus** — may be cardiogenic shock. Call ICU.
- **Arrhythmia** — new onset atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia, or heart block. Call ICU.

**Specific thresholds (make them concrete):**

| Parameter            | Threshold for escalation                                                                 |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| NEWS2                | ≥5 (or any single parameter RED) — call ICU for review                                  |
| Lactate              | >4 mmol/L (signs of hypoperfusion)                                                       |
| GCS                  | ≤13 (consider meningitis, cerebral vasculitis)                                           |
| Blood pressure       | <5th centile for age (hypotension) — call ICU; start fluid resuscitation, inotropes if needed |
| Fever duration       | >10 days without treatment — risk of aneurysm is very high, start IVIG immediately even if echo normal |
| Oxygen saturation    | <92% (possible myocarditis or ARDS)                                                      |
| Chest pain           | Any — get ECG + echo + troponin; if ST elevation, call cardiology                        |
| Rash progression     | New purpura or petechiae — consider other vasculitides, thrombocytopenia, DIC            |

**Bottom line:** If the child looks unwell, is not responding to IVIG, or has any suggestion

# Kawasaki Disease

**One-liner:** Acute, self-limited vasculitis of medium-sized arteries, primarily in children <5 years, causing coronary artery aneurysms if untreated.

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 3-year-old boy presents with day 6 of high spiking fever (39.5°C) unresponsive to paracetamol and ibuprofen. Mum notes he has been irritable, refusing food, and has red, cracked lips. On examination you find bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, a polymorphous truncal rash, erythema of the palms and soles with mild swelling, and a single 2 cm right anterior cervical lymph node. The child is miserable, and the parents are exhausted.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues and what they tell you:**

- **Fever ≥5 days (day 6 here):** This is the *sine qua non* — no fever, no Kawasaki. The duration reflects ongoing systemic inflammation; fever persisting beyond 5 days is the diagnostic gateway.
- **Irritability out of proportion:** Suggests CNS inflammation (aseptic meningitis) or severe general malaise. In a febrile child, extreme irritability is a red flag for vasculitis.
- **Red, cracked lips and strawberry tongue:** Mucosal involvement — hyperaemia of oral mucosa, prominent papillae. The vasculitis targets small- to medium-sized vessels in mucous membranes.
- **Bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection:** Not "pink eye" (no discharge, no crusting). Vasculitis of bulbar conjunctival vessels; hints at systemic endothelial activation.
- **Polymorphous rash:** Often maculopapular, can be scarlatiniform or target-like. Not diagnostic alone, but supports acute inflammation.
- **Erythema and swelling of palms/soles:** "Strawberry hands and feet" — acute-phase vasodilation and oedema. In subacute phase (weeks 2-3), you'll see periungual desquamation.
- **Single enlarged cervical node >1.5 cm:** Usually non-suppurative, unilateral. Least common criterion (present in ~50-75%). The node is a red herring for bacterial lymphadenitis, but cultures are negative.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Incomplete Kawasaki Disease (IKD):** Fever ≥5 days but only 2-3 of the other 5 criteria. Commonest in infants <6 months and older children >5 years. Look for supporting labs (elevated CRP, ESR, low albumin, sterile pyuria) and echocardiogram changes.
- **Infant <6 months:** May present only with fever and irritability, no classic signs at all. High silent coronary aneurysm rate — **echocardiogram is mandatory** for any febrile infant with no source and high inflammatory markers.
- **Older child (>5 years):** More likely to have atypical features like cervical lymphadenopathy mimicking bacterial infection, or abdominal pain from vasculitis of mesenteric vessels. They also have higher rates of IVIG resistance.

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Kawasaki disease (KD) is an acute, self-limited inflammation of blood vessel walls (vasculitis) that specifically targets the coronary arteries. It mostly strikes young children, and if not treated within 10 days of fever onset, it can cause balloon-like enlargements (aneurysms) in the heart's arteries.

**Why it matters clinically:**  

- **Frequency:** Leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in developed countries (after congenital heart disease). Incidence ~20/100,000 in Japan, ~9-19/100,000 in UK/US, with peak at 1-2 years.
- **Stakes:** Untreated, 15-25% develop coronary artery aneurysms; even with treatment, risk falls to ~4%. Aneurysms can thrombose → myocardial infarction, rupture, or cause ischaemic heart disease in young adults.
- **Common pitfalls:**  
  - Misdiagnosing as viral exanthem, scarlet fever, or measles → delayed treatment.  
  - Missing incomplete KD in infants → silent aneurysms.  
  - Forgetting that fever must be **documented** for 5 days (history counts).  
  - Not arranging early echocardiogram (day 7-10 is optimal, but can be earlier).

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of how KD develops:**

KD is triggered by an unknown antigen (likely a microbial superantigen) in a genetically susceptible host (certain HLA types, Asian ethnicity). The antigen activates T cells and macrophages → massive cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-8) → systemic inflammation.

Vascular endothelium expresses adhesion molecules, attracting neutrophils and later macrophages. In medium-sized arteries (especially coronaries), neutrophils infiltrate the vessel wall and release proteases, causing destruction of the internal elastic lamina and smooth muscle. This weakens the vessel wall → dilatation → aneurysm.

**Why each clinical sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

- **Fever:** Cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) reset the hypothalamic setpoint → sustained high body temperature.
- **Conjunctival injection:** Vasodilation and inflammation of bulbar conjunctival vessels (no discharge because it's not an infection of the conjunctival epithelium).
- **Mucosal changes (red lips, strawberry tongue):** Vasculitis of oral mucosal vessels causes hyperaemia, oedema, and later desquamation. The tongue's papillae become prominent due to oedema and inflammation.
- **Rash:** Dermal small-vessel vasculitis → polymorphous exanthem. Not specific, but the pattern (truncal, perineal accentuation) hints at KD.
- **Extremity changes (erythema, oedema, desquamation):** Acute: vasodilation and dermal oedema from cytokine-mediated capillary leak. Subacute (weeks 2-3): ischaemia of the superficial epidermis due to small-vessel involvement → periungual desquamation.
- **Cervical lymphadenopathy:** Drainage from inflamed pharyngeal or upper respiratory mucosa; the node is reactive, not suppurative.
- **Irritability:** CNS vasculitis or meningeal inflammation (CSF shows pleocytosis in ~30%).
- **Coronary aneurysms:** Medium-sized artery wall destruction. The same process can affect other medium arteries (axillary, renal, mesenteric) but coronaries are the clinical focus.

**Connect pathophys to pattern-recognition cues:**  

- Fever ≥5 days = sustained cytokine release (not a typical viral pattern that resolves by day 3-4).  
- Bilateral conjunctivitis without discharge = endothelial activation, not bacterial conjunctivitis (which is purulent).  
- Strawberry tongue = mucosal vasculitis, not Group A Strep pharyngitis (which is exudative and painful).  
- Swollen hands/feet = capillary leak + local vasodilation, not oedema from renal or cardiac causes (no proteinuria or heart failure).  
- Irritability = CNS involvement → think beyond viral meningitis (no photophobia or neck stiffness typical of viral or bacterial).

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category        | Specific Trigger / Factor                         | Stem Clue                                                    | Most Common      |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic**  | Unknown, but thought to be infection-triggered in genetically predisposed | No clear contact; child otherwise well; starts abruptly       | **Yes — over 90% of cases** |
| **Infectious**  | Superantigen-producing pathogens (Staph, Strep, Mycoplasma, EBV, Adenovirus) | Prodromal URI or GI symptoms; clustering in winter/spring; sometimes sibling cases | May be co-trigger, not single cause |
| **Genetic**     | HLA class II polymorphisms, ITPKC gene variants   | Asian ethnicity (especially Japanese, Korean); family history | MODIFIER, not cause |
| **Environmental**| Possibly carpet shampoo, mercury, detergents (weak evidence) | Rare; historical reports only                                  | Not clinically useful |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **idiopathic** — you will almost never find a single pathogen. Just treat empirically.

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (American Heart Association, 2017):**  

- **Classic KD:** Fever ≥5 days + at least 4 of 5 principal clinical features:  
  1. Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection  
  2. Oral changes (red lips, strawberry tongue, pharyngeal erythema)  
  3. Polymorphous rash  
  4. Extremity changes (erythema/oedema of palms/soles; later periungual desquamation)  
  5. Cervical lymphadenopathy >1.5 cm (usually unilateral)  

- **Incomplete KD:** Fever ≥5 days + <4 features, but **echocardiogram shows coronary changes** OR lab findings (elevated CRP/ESR + ≥3 supplemental lab criteria).  

**Supplemental lab criteria:**  
- Albumin ≤3.0 g/dL  
- Anaemia for age  
- ALT >50 U/L  
- Platelets ≥450 x 10⁹/L after day 7 (thrombocytosis is *late* — not helpful acutely)  
- WBC ≥15 x 10⁹/L  
- Urine WBC >10/hpf (sterile pyuria)

**Pattern-recognition:** No single lab confirms KD; diagnosis is clinical + lab support. But **echo is the gold standard** – if you see coronary artery dilation or aneurysms, you have KD regardless of criteria.

| Test            | What It Shows                                           | Why Order                 | How to Interpret                                                                                                                                 |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| CRP             | Highly elevated >30-100 mg/L (normal <5)                 | Marker of systemic inflammation | KD typically has very high CRP (often >70). Falling CRP after IVIG is a good sign.                                                              |
| ESR             | Elevated >40 mm/hr (can be >100)                        | Same, but slower          | ESR may remain high for weeks; not useful for monitoring acute response.                                                                          |
| WBC with diff   | Leukocytosis, neutrophilia                              | Acute-phase               | >15 is supportive, but not specific.                                                                                                             |
| Platelets       | Normal in first week; **thrombocytosis** weeks 2-3       | Late diagnostic clue       | Platelets >450 after day 7 is highly suggestive. If you see this, re-evaluate for missed KD.                                                     |
| Albumin         | Low (<3.0 g/dL)                                         | Capillary leak             | Hypoalbuminaemia is common in KD (loss into tissues). Supports diagnosis.                                                                        |
| LFT (ALT)       | Mildly elevated (AST/ALT 2-3x normal)                   | Hepatic involvement        | Transaminitis is common (~40%). Suggests systemic vasculitis.                                                                                    |
| Urinalysis      | Sterile pyuria (>10 WBC/hpf, no bacteria)                | Rule out UTI               | Indicates renal vasculitis. If positive, don't treat as UTI – consider KD.                                                                       |
| Echo (echocardiogram) | Coronary artery Z-scores (diameter adjusted for BSA) | **Definitive** for aneurysms | Z-score ≥2.5 = aneurysm. Repeat at 1-2 weeks, then 6-8 weeks after onset.                                                                        |

**Single finding that confirms KD:**  
No single test — but a **coronary artery aneurysm on echocardiogram** in a child with fever ≥5 days is pathognomonic. Most cases don't have aneurysms at presentation, so the clinical criteria and labs guide early treatment.

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **IVIG** (2 g/kg as single IV infusion over 10-12 hours) — reduces coronary aneurysm rate from 25% to 4% if given within 10 days of fever onset. Mechanism: anti-inflammatory, neutralises superantigens, downregulates cytokines.  
- **Aspirin** — high-dose initially (30-50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h) until afebrile for 48-72 hours. Anti-inflammatory dose. Start at same time as IVIG.  
- **Paracetamol** — if fever high (but don't mask response to IVIG).  
- **Check IVIG resistance risk factors:** severe hypoalbuminaemia, very high CRP, low platelets, elevated bilirubin. If high risk, consider adding corticosteroids early (discuss with senior).

**Ward (24-72 hours):**  
- **Monitor for defervescence** — if fever persists ≥36 hours after IVIG, this is **IVIG resistance** (occurring in ~10-20%). Needs second dose IVIG ± methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg IV for 1-3 days (or infliximab 5 mg/kg IV).  
- **Transition aspirin to low-dose** (3-5 mg/kg/day once daily) once child is afebrile and CRP falling. Low-dose aspirin for antiplatelet effect — decreases thrombosis risk on any developing aneurysm.  
- **Echocardiogram** — ideally day 7-10 after onset (not day 1, because aneurysms take time to develop). Earlier if critically ill.  
- **Daily monitoring of CRP, clinical signs, fluid balance.** KD kids are often dehydrated from fever and poor intake.

**Long-term:**  
- **Aspirin (low-dose) continued** until echocardiogram at 6-8 weeks shows normal coronaries. If normal, stop aspirin. If aneurysm persists, continue aspirin indefinitely ± warfarin if giant aneurysm (>8 mm).  
- **Cardiology follow-up** — serial EKGs, stress tests, angiography if aneurysms present.  
- **Live vaccines** — defer MMR/varicella for 11 months after IVIG (circulating antibodies interfere).  
- **Lifelong risk** — even regressed aneurysms may have impaired vasodilation; adult risk of accelerated atherosclerosis.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**  
- **Do NOT use corticosteroids as first-line** — they increase aneurysm risk in KD (except in IVIG-resistant cases, or as adjunct in high-risk patients).  
- **Do NOT stop aspirin early** — antiplatelet effect needed for 6-8 weeks even with normal echo (may have subtle endothelial dysfunction).  
- **Do NOT give NSAIDs (ibuprofen)** — compete with aspirin for COX-1 binding, reduce antiplatelet effect. Use paracetamol.  
- **Do NOT use IVIG if >10 days of fever and no echo evidence of aneurysm** — but if aneurysm present, still give IVIG to reduce inflammation.  
- **Do NOT forget echo** — a normal echo at day 7 does not rule out later aneurysm; repeat at 6-8 weeks.

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature          | Kawasaki Disease (KD)      | Scarlet Fever (Group A Strep) | Measles                  | Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA) – systemic onset | Viral Exanthem (e.g., Adenovirus) |
|------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Pain**         | Irritability, no localised pain | Sore throat, headache        | Cough, coryza, photophobia | Arthralgia/arthritis (can be migratory)              | Malignant fever, aches            |
| **Exam**         | Conjunctivitis (non-purulent), red lips, strawberry tongue, palmar/plantar erythema, rash, unilateral cervical node | Circumoral pallor, strawberry tongue (early), red pharynx with exudate, sandpaper rash, Pastia lines, desquamation later | Three Cs: Cough, Coryza, Conjunctivitis (purulent). Koplik spots (buccal, white). Rash: confluent, red, starting on face. | High quotidian fever (once or twice daily), evanescent salmon-pink rash, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly | Pharyngitis, tonsillitis, exudates sometimes; follicular conjunctivitis only. No strawberry tongue or palmar changes. |
| **Oral findings** | Red cracked lips, strawberry tongue, pharyngeal erythema without exudates | Strawberry tongue (early white, later red), exudative pharyngitis | Koplik spots (white on red base, buccal), then red throat | Usually normal oral mucosa | Pharyngeal injection, vesicles/herpangina possible |
| **Extremities**  | Palmar/plantar erythema, oedema, **periungual desquamation** (weeks 2-3) | Desquamation (late, around flexures, fingers) | No specific extremity findings | Joint swelling, warmth, limited range | No specific extremity changes |
| **Lymph nodes**  | Single anterior cervical node >1.5 cm, non-suppurative | Tender, suppurative cervical nodes | Posterior auricular, occipital nodes | Generalised adenopathy | Cervical nodes, often bilateral, may be reactive |
| **ECG**          | May show myocarditis (ST changes), arrhythmias | Normal              | Normal                  | Pericarditis/myocarditis possible                    | Normal                          |
| **Labs**         | High CRP, high ESR, low albumin, thrombocytosis (later), sterile pyuria | ASOT, anti-DNAse B positive, culture + throat | Measles IgM/IgG, viral PCR | Anaemia, high CRP/ESR, ANA/ RF negative usually | Viral PCR, serology; low CRP often |
| **Key differentiator** | Fever ≥5 days + 4/5 criteria + coronary aneurysm on echo | Strep culture + ASOT; responds to penicillin | Koplik spots + measles PCR; prodrome + face-first rash | Arthritis + typical quotidian fever + rash; responds to NSAIDs/systemic steroids | Self-limited (3-5 days), normal echo, no conjunctivitis or oral changes typical |

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complication #1: Coronary artery aneurysms (CAA)**  
- **How it presents:** Often asymptomatic initially. On echo: z-score ≥2.5 (mild) to giant aneurysm (≥8 mm). Giant aneurysms may present later as ischaemia, dysrhythmia, or sudden death.  
- **Key signs:** None until thrombotic event: chest pain, pallor, syncope, cardiogenic shock in a child with history of fever weeks ago. ECG: ST elevation, Q waves (myocardial infarction).  
- **What to do:** Urgent echo, ECG, troponin. If acute MI, manage per paediatric cardiology protocol (thrombolysis or angioplasty tricky in children). Immediate heparin, consult cardiology.  

**Major complication #2: Myocarditis**  
- **How it presents:** Heart failure (tachypnoea, gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, hypotension) within first week.  
- **Key signs:** Tachycardia out of proportion to fever, pulsus alternans, raised JVP.  
- **What to do:** ECG (low voltage, ST/T changes), echo (reduced ejection fraction), treat with IVIG, diuretics, inotropes (ICU).  

**Major complication #3: Aseptic meningitis**  
- **How it presents:** Irritability, photophobia, headache.  
- **Key signs:** Meningism but CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, negative cultures.  
- **What to do:** Lumbar puncture (to rule out bacterial meningitis). Treat underlying KD — resolves with IVIG.  

**Major complication #4: Peripheral arterial aneurysms**  
- **How it presents:** Pulsatile masses in axilla, groin, or abdomen. Rare but can rupture.  
- **What to do:** Vascular surgery referral.  

**Late-presentation patterns (the STEMs testing "what happens if untreated"):**  
- "A 7-year-old child presents with syncope while running. Had a prolonged febrile illness at age 2 not fully evaluated." — Think old Kawasaki with giant aneurysm causing exertion-induced ischaemia.  
- "A 20-year-old adult has an MI with no risk factors. Echocardiogram shows a calcified coronary aneurysm." — Sequelae of undiagnosed KD in childhood.

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **"CRASH"** — the 5 clinical features (non-fever criteria):  
   - **C**onjunctivitis (bilateral, non-purulent)  
   - **R**ash (polymorphous, truncal)  
   - **A**denopathy (cervical, >1.5 cm, unilateral)  
   - **S**trawberry tongue / oral changes  
   - **H**ands/feet changes (erythema, oedema, later desquamation)  

2. **"5 days of fever, 4 of 5 signs"** — diagnostic threshold for classic KD.

3. **"Ideal to IVIG before day 10"** — give IVIG ideally day 7-10; after day 10, still give if echo abnormal.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

- In a febrile child with no source, **always ask about conjunctival injection and check palms/ soles** — the classic exam is frequently missed.
- **Periungual desquamation** (fingertip peeling) appears **after** fever resolves, weeks 2-3. If you see this in a child with previous febrile illness, ask about the history and echo retrospectively.
- **Thrombocytosis** (>450) after day 7 is a strong clue for KD. If you see it in a febrile child with no clear diagnosis, think of incomplete KD.
- **Sterile pyuria** in a febrile child with negative urine culture — suspect KD, not UTI.
- **Age extremes:** Infants <6 months and children >5 years are atypical presentations — lower threshold for echo.
- **High-risk for IVIG resistance:** Asian male, age <1 year, low albumin, low sodium, high CRP, elevated bilirubin. Consider adding corticosteroid early.
- **Do not use ibuprofen** in KD — it competes with aspirin for COX-1 and reduces antiplatelet efficacy. Paracetamol is safe for fever.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Diagnosing scarlet fever without culture — but KD also has strawberry tongue. Look for conjunctival injection (absent in scarlet fever) and palmar oedema.
- Stopping aspirin after fever resolves — continue low-dose until 6-8 week echo confirms normal coronaries.
- Missing incomplete KD: fever + 2-3 criteria + abnormal labs = treat. Don't wait for full criteria.
- Forgetting that IVIG contains live antibody → live vaccines delayed 11 months.

## 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / cardiology / ICU:**

- **Persistent fever >36 hours after first IVIG dose** — likely IVIG resistance; needs repeat IVIG ± steroids/infliximab. Call paediatric rheumatologist/cardiologist.
- **Signs of myocarditis or heart failure** — tachypnoea, tachycardia out of proportion, gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, low urine output, hypotension. Call ICU and cardiology.
- **Chest pain or syncope** — suspect myocardial ischaemia from aneurysm. Call cardiology emergently.
- **Hypotension not responding to bolus** — may be cardiogenic shock. Call ICU.
- **Arrhythmia** — new onset atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia, or heart block. Call ICU.

**Specific thresholds (make them concrete):**

| Parameter            | Threshold for escalation                                                                 |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| NEWS2                | ≥5 (or any single parameter RED) — call ICU for review                                  |
| Lactate              | >4 mmol/L (signs of hypoperfusion)                                                       |
| GCS                  | ≤13 (consider meningitis, cerebral vasculitis)                                           |
| Blood pressure       | <5th centile for age (hypotension) — call ICU; start fluid resuscitation, inotropes if needed |
| Fever duration       | >10 days without treatment — risk of aneurysm is very high, start IVIG immediately even if echo normal |
| Oxygen saturation    | <92% (possible myocarditis or ARDS)                                                      |
| Chest pain           | Any — get ECG + echo + troponin; if ST elevation, call cardiology                        |
| Rash progression     | New purpura or petechiae — consider other vasculitides, thrombocytopenia, DIC            |

**Bottom line:** If the child looks unwell, is not responding to IVIG, or has any suggestion
